title: Rate Limiting & Backpressure
description: When demand outruns capacity, a system either sheds load gracefully or collapses. This quiz walks the full path: the algorithms (token vs. leaky bucket, fixed vs. sliding window), where to enforce a limit (per-IP vs. per-user, client-side debounce/throttle), how to communicate it (`429`, `Retry-After`, backoff with jitter), and how backpressure propagates through Node.js streams so a slow consumer doesn't exhaust memory. Every answer is computed, executed, or cited against the relevant spec.

questions.title: Token bucket vs. leaky bucket: which one lets traffic burst?
questions.description: Both algorithms cap the long-run average rate, but they shape short-term traffic differently.

options.text: **Token bucket** lets traffic burst up to the bucket's capacity, then settles to the refill rate; **leaky bucket** drains at a fixed rate, smoothing bursts away
**Leaky bucket** lets traffic burst up to its capacity; **token bucket** forces a perfectly constant output rate
Both shape output into a perfectly constant rate and forbid any burst
Both allow unlimited bursts as long as the long-run average stays under the limit

questions.explanation:
A **token bucket** accumulates tokens at a fixed refill rate up to a maximum (its *capacity*). A request spends one token and passes immediately if a token is available. After an idle stretch the bucket is full, so a client can fire a burst as large as the capacity all at once — and only *then* is it throttled down to the refill rate. Mnemonic: *save up, spend in a burst.*

A **leaky bucket** models a bucket with a hole: requests pour in (overflowing if it's full) but *leave* at a constant rate, so the output is smooth no matter how bursty the input. Mnemonic: *constant trickle.*

So token bucket tolerates bursts; leaky bucket erases them. The reversed and "both identical" options are the classic confusions. (Note: some texts describe a *leaky-bucket-as-a-meter* variant that behaves like a token bucket; the burst-vs-smooth contrast here uses the well-known *leaky-bucket-as-a-queue* model.)
questions.references: [Stripe: Scaling your API with rate limiters](https://stripe.com/blog/rate-limiters) · [Wikipedia: Token bucket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Token_bucket)

questions.title: How many requests succeed in that first instantaneous burst?
questions.description: A token bucket holds at most **20** tokens and refills at **5** tokens per second; each request costs 1 token. After the bucket has sat idle long enough to fill completely, a client suddenly fires many requests at once.
questions.explanation:
The bucket's **capacity is the burst ceiling**. While idle, tokens accrue at 5/s but are capped at 20, so a full bucket holds exactly 20 tokens. An *instantaneous* burst means no time passes, so the refill adds nothing — the client can spend all 20 tokens at once, and **20** requests pass immediately.

The 21st request must wait for a refill. From there the client is throttled to the sustained rate: 5 tokens/sec = one request every 200 ms. This is the entire design intent — *capacity* sets how big a burst you tolerate, *refill rate* sets the sustained throughput. (A leaky-bucket limiter with the same 5/s average would not allow this burst; it would release requests at a steady 5 per second.)

questions.title: What does the fixed-window limiter do with these 200 requests?
questions.description: A fixed-window limiter allows **100 requests per minute**, counted against wall-clock minutes (the counter resets at `:00`). A client sends 100 requests at `12:00:59.9` and another 100 at `12:01:00.1`.

options.text: About 200 requests are admitted within roughly one second — the limiter is briefly fooled into allowing ~2× the intended rate near the window edge
All 200 are rejected, because the limiter sums them to 200 over the minute
The second batch is automatically deferred to the `12:02` window
Only 100 total are admitted; fixed windows track a rolling 60-second total

questions.explanation:
The fixed window counts against the *clock*, not against each request. At `12:00:59.9` the `12:00` window still has room, so 100 requests pass. Just 0.2 s later the clock ticks to `12:01`, the counter **resets to 0**, and 100 more pass. Net result: ~200 requests in ~1 second — double the intended ceiling — even though each one-minute window individually counted only 100.

This "boundary burst" is the headline weakness of fixed windows, and it's exactly what sliding-window algorithms fix by measuring a window that moves *with the current request* instead of snapping to the clock. The distractors describe sliding-window or queueing behavior, not fixed windows.
questions.references: [Cloudflare: How we built rate limiting capable of scaling to millions of domains](https://blog.cloudflare.com/counting-things-a-lot-of-different-things/)

questions.title: What is the estimated request count for the trailing 60 seconds?
questions.description:
A sliding-window-*counter* limiter uses 60-second windows. Right now you are **15 seconds** into the current window. The **previous** window saw **80** requests; the **current** window has logged **20** so far.

Use the standard weighted estimate `estimate = current + previous × (window − elapsed) / window`; round to the nearest whole request.
questions.explanation:
Plug in the numbers. The weight on the previous window is `(60 − 15) / 60 = 45 / 60 = 0.75`, so:

`estimate = 20 + 80 × 0.75 = 20 + 60 = `**`80`**`.

The intuition: a *true* sliding window would count every request in the trailing 60 s, which needs a timestamp per request. The counter approximates by assuming the previous window's requests were spread evenly, so it keeps only the fraction still inside the trailing 60 s — here 45 of its 60 seconds, i.e. 75% — and adds everything in the current window. Against a limit of 100, this request is admitted (80 < 100). Had the previous window held 110, the estimate would be `20 + 110 × 0.75 = 102.5` → blocked, even though the current window alone is far under 100. The payoff is **O(1) memory per key** (two integers) instead of the exact log's O(n).
questions.references: [Cloudflare: How we built rate limiting capable of scaling to millions of domains](https://blog.cloudflare.com/counting-things-a-lot-of-different-things/)

questions.title: What's the difference between a sliding-window log and counter?
questions.description: You must hold per-key limiter state for tens of millions of API keys in Redis, and you're choosing between two sliding-window implementations.

options.text: The **log** stores a timestamp per request (exact, but O(n) memory per key); the **counter** keeps two integers per key (O(1) memory, but an approximation)
The **counter** is exact; the **log** is only an approximation that drifts over time
Both store one timestamp per request and differ only in their eviction policy
The **log** uses constant memory; the **counter** grows with the request rate

questions.explanation:
A sliding-window **log** keeps the timestamp of every request (e.g. a Redis sorted set), counts the entries newer than `now − window`, and trims the rest. It is perfectly accurate — but its memory and trim cost grow with the number of requests in the window, **O(n) per key**, which is punishing across tens of millions of keys.

A sliding-window **counter** keeps only the current and previous fixed-window totals — two integers — and blends them with the weighted estimate from the previous question. That's **O(1) memory** and one arithmetic step, at the cost of being an approximation (it assumes the previous window's load was uniform). At scale the counter's memory win dominates; reserve the log for cases that truly need exactness. The distractors simply invert which structure is exact and which is constant-memory.
questions.references: [Cloudflare: How we built rate limiting capable of scaling to millions of domains](https://blog.cloudflare.com/counting-things-a-lot-of-different-things/)

questions.title: What's the real trade-off between per-IP and per-user rate limiting?
questions.description: You're deciding what to key a limiter on: the source IP, or the authenticated user / API key.

options.text: Per-IP can collectively throttle many distinct users sharing one address (corporate NAT, carrier-grade NAT, a VPN exit); per-user keying is fairer but needs to identify the caller, so it can't gate fully anonymous traffic
Per-IP is strictly fairer, because every user is guaranteed a unique IP address
Per-user limiting needs no authentication and behaves identically for anonymous and signed-in callers
Per-IP fully stops abuse, because an attacker cannot change their source IP

questions.explanation:
The key decides *who shares a bucket*. Per-IP is cheap and works for anonymous traffic, but many unrelated users routinely share one public IP — office and campus NATs, carrier-grade NAT on mobile networks, shared VPN/proxy exits — so one heavy user (or one abuser) can drain the limit for everyone behind that address.

Per-user / per-API-key keying gives each account its own budget regardless of network: fairer and more precise, but it presupposes you can *identify* the caller, so it doesn't help before login. And neither is a silver bullet against abuse — attackers rotate through botnets and proxy pools, so per-IP limits alone don't "stop" them. Production systems usually **layer both**: a coarse per-IP limit for anonymous/edge protection plus a per-key limit for authenticated quotas.

questions.title: 429 vs. 503: which says "it's you," which says "it's me"?
questions.description: A gateway must reject traffic in two different situations: (a) one client blew past its own per-key quota, and (b) the whole upstream is overloaded and shedding load for everyone.

options.text: (a) → `429 Too Many Requests` (this client is over its limit); (b) → `503 Service Unavailable` (the server can't cope right now). Both may carry `Retry-After`
(a) → `503 Service Unavailable`; (b) → `429 Too Many Requests` — the codes map to server vs. client load in that order
Both situations should return `403 Forbidden`, since the request is being refused
Both should return `200 OK` with an error body, so clients don't treat it as a failure

questions.explanation:
`429 Too Many Requests` (RFC 6585 §4) means *this client* sent too many requests in a given time — the fault is attributable to the caller's rate. `503 Service Unavailable` (RFC 9110 §15.6.4) means *the server* is temporarily unable to handle the request — overloaded, deploying, or shedding load for everyone. Both are designed to carry **`Retry-After`** to tell the client when to come back.

Why the distractors fail: `403 Forbidden` is an authorization verdict ("you may never do this"), not "slow down" — retrying a 403 unchanged is pointless. And tunneling failures through `200 OK` is the anti-pattern that blinds every proxy, cache, retry library, and dashboard that branches on the status code. Use the status line to say *what kind* of failure this is; use `Retry-After` to say *when* to retry.

questions.title: Which are valid `Retry-After` values?
questions.description: Per RFC 9110 §10.2.3, `Retry-After`'s value is **either** an HTTP-date **or** a non-negative number of seconds. Select **all** that a spec-compliant server may send.

options.text: `Retry-After: 120` — wait 120 seconds (delay-seconds)
`Retry-After: Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT` — an absolute HTTP-date
`Retry-After: 2m0s` — a Go-style duration string
`Retry-After: now+30` — a relative expression

questions.explanation:
RFC 9110 defines `Retry-After = HTTP-date / delay-seconds`. So a bare non-negative integer (`120`, meaning "wait 120 seconds") is valid, and an absolute IMF-fixdate such as `Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT` is valid.

What's **not** valid: programming-language or ad-hoc duration syntaxes. `2m0s` is Go's `time.Duration` format and `now+30` is invented — HTTP defines neither, so a conforming client isn't required to parse them and will typically ignore the header, falling back to its own backoff. When you emit `Retry-After`, send integer seconds *or* a correctly formatted HTTP-date — nothing else.

questions.title: What is the delay, in milliseconds, before the retry at `attempt = 4`?
questions.description: A client retries on `429` with exponential backoff and **no** jitter: `delay = base × 2^attempt`, where `base = 100` ms and `attempt` starts at `0` for the first retry. The server keeps returning `429`.
questions.explanation:
Substitute: `delay = 100 × 2^4 = 100 × 16 = `**`1600`**` ms`.

The per-attempt sequence is `100, 200, 400, 800, 1600` ms for attempts 0–4 — each retry waits twice as long as the last, so the client backs off quickly while failures persist. Two refinements you'd add in production:

- a **cap**, `min(cap, base × 2^attempt)`, so the delay can't grow without bound, and
- **jitter**, randomizing within `[0, delay]`, so many clients don't retry in lockstep.

AWS's "full jitter" recipe folds both together: `sleep = random(0, min(cap, base × 2^attempt))`.

questions.title: Which statements about adding jitter to backoff are correct?
questions.description: After a spike of `429`/`503` responses, thousands of clients all retry with exponential backoff. Adding *jitter* randomizes each client's delay. Select **all** that apply.

options.text: It de-synchronizes retries so clients don't all fire at the same instant, avoiding a self-inflicted "thundering herd" that re-overloads the recovering server
It spreads the retry load across an interval, smoothing the spike the server has to absorb
It guarantees every individual client a shorter wait than plain exponential backoff
It makes each client's retry schedule deterministic and identical, which is easier to reason about

questions.explanation:
Without jitter, clients that failed at the same moment compute the *same* backoff (2 s, then 4 s, then 8 s…) and retry in synchronized waves, each wave hammering the server just as it tries to recover. Jitter breaks the synchronization by randomizing each delay (e.g. AWS "full jitter": `sleep = random(0, min(cap, base × 2^attempt))`), so retries arrive spread across the interval instead of all at once — fewer collisions, lower peak load.

The two false options invert what jitter does: it does **not** shorten any individual client's wait (on a given roll a client may wait *longer*), and it deliberately *introduces* randomness, so schedules become non-deterministic — that's the mechanism, not a drawback. The goal is lower contention and peak load, not lower per-client latency.

questions.title: What is the common two-word name for this synchronized-retry problem?
questions.description: Many clients fail at the same moment, all wait the *same* fixed backoff, then retry in a single synchronized wave that knocks the server over again just as it recovers — repeating the cycle. Adding jitter is the standard cure.
questions.explanation:
This is the **thundering herd** problem: a large group of clients (or processes, or threads) all wake or retry at the same instant and stampede a shared resource together, so the recovering server is toppled again before it can stabilize. Fixed-delay retries cause it because everyone computes the identical next-attempt time.

The cure is to spread the herd out: add **jitter** (randomize each backoff within `[0, delay]`) on top of exponential backoff and a retry cap. The same term appears in OS scheduling (many threads woken by one event contend for a lock) and in caching, where a popular key expires and every request misses to the origin at once — a *cache stampede*, a sibling of the same idea.

questions.title: What does a `false` return from `writable.write()` mean?

options.text: The internal buffer has reached `highWaterMark`. The chunk was still accepted and queued, but you should pause and wait for the `'drain'` event before writing more
The write failed; the chunk was dropped and you must send it again
The chunk exceeded `highWaterMark` and was rejected; split it into smaller pieces
The stream has already ended, so no further writes are possible

questions.title: What happens when the source far outpaces the slow socket?

options.text: Node keeps accepting and buffering the unflushed chunks in memory; the writable's buffer grows without bound until memory is exhausted and the process crashes
`write()` blocks the event loop until each chunk is flushed, so memory stays flat but throughput collapses
Node detects the imbalance and automatically pauses the source for you
The excess chunks are silently discarded once the buffer passes `highWaterMark`, so memory is safe

questions.explanation:
`highWaterMark` is advisory, not a hard limit. When you ignore the `false` return from `write()` and keep pushing, Node neither blocks nor drops data — it queues every chunk in the writable's internal buffer, which grows unbounded because the producer outpaces the consumer. Memory climbs until the process is OOM-killed — the exact failure backpressure exists to prevent.

The distractors are three tempting myths: `write()` is **non-blocking** (it returns immediately, so it can't throttle by stalling the loop); Node does **not** auto-pause a *manually* driven source — only `pipe()` / `pipeline()` wire up that handshake; and chunks past the watermark are **buffered, never silently dropped**. The fix is to honor the `'drain'` signal yourself or, better, hand the whole transfer to `stream.pipeline(fastSource, slowSocket, cb)`, which propagates backpressure, errors, and cleanup end to end.

questions.title: Why does a bounded queue beat an unbounded one under overload?
questions.description: A service receives work faster than it can process it for a sustained period. One design buffers everything in an **unbounded** in-memory queue; another keeps a **bounded** queue and rejects (sheds) work once it's full.

options.text: The unbounded queue turns sustained overload into ever-growing latency and memory until the process OOMs — and by the time old items run, callers have often timed out. A bounded queue + load shedding fails fast (e.g. `429`/`503`), keeping the system responsive
The unbounded queue is safe because, by Little's Law, a longer queue raises throughput above the service rate
The bounded queue is worse: rejecting requests always lowers total goodput compared with eventually serving them all
Queue length has no effect on latency as long as the average arrival rate equals the average service rate

questions.explanation:
Throughput is capped by the **service rate** μ, not by how big the queue is — a bigger buffer adds no processing power. If arrivals λ exceed μ for any sustained period, the backlog grows at roughly `(λ − μ)` per second without bound. **Little's Law** (`L = λ × W`) captures the consequence: the more items are in the system (L), the longer each one waits (W). So an unbounded queue converts overload into *unbounded latency* — requests sit so long that clients time out, and you burn capacity computing responses nobody is waiting for (negative goodput) — and *unbounded memory*, until the process is OOM-killed.

**Load shedding** is the deliberate fix: cap the queue and reject the overflow immediately with a fast `429`/`503` + `Retry-After`, so accepted work stays fresh and the server protects its own stability — *fail fast* beats *queue forever*. The claim that queue length stops mattering once λ equals μ hides a subtlety: even when λ ≈ μ on average, bursts make the queue and latency fluctuate, and as utilization approaches 100% the queueing delay grows sharply.

questions.title: Which of these two behaviors is debounce, and which is throttle?
questions.description: Two client-side rate-control techniques for noisy event streams (keystrokes, scroll, resize): one fires a single time *after* activity settles; the other fires at most once per fixed interval *during* activity.

options.text: **Debounce** waits for a quiet gap and then fires once (ideal for search-as-you-type, where you only query after the user pauses); **throttle** fires at most once per interval during continuous events (ideal for scroll/resize handlers)
**Throttle** fires once after activity stops; **debounce** fires at a steady cadence during activity
Both fire on every event but delay each callback by a fixed amount
They are interchangeable names for the same timer-based technique

questions.explanation:
Both collapse a flood of events into fewer calls, but with different timing. **Debounce** resets a timer on every event and only fires when events *stop* for the chosen interval — so a burst of 50 keystrokes triggers exactly one call, after the user pauses. Perfect for search-as-you-type or "resize finished" work.

**Throttle** instead enforces a steady ceiling: at most one call per interval *while events keep coming* — so a scroll firing hundreds of events per second is reduced to, say, one handler call every 100 ms. Rule of thumb: *debounce* when you only care about the final state after activity ends; *throttle* when you want regular updates during activity but capped in frequency. They're the client-side cousins of server rate limiting — same goal (protect a resource from too-frequent calls), applied in the browser before a request is even sent.
